item.title: Rep. Johnny Olszewski proposes bill for 18-year Supreme Court term limits
item.description: Recent polling released by NBC News shows that only 22% of Americans have confidence in the U.S. Supreme Court. In the wake of that growing unpopularity, a new proposal in Congress would amend the Constitution to impose 18-year term limits on Supreme Court justices. Democratic Rep. Johnny Olszewski of Maryland joins "The Takeout" to discuss.

item.title: Border czar Tom Homan says Trump administration isn't backing down on mass deportations
item.description: White House border czar Tom Homan sat down for an exclusive interview with CBS News immigration and politics correspondent Camilo Montoya-Galvez on Tuesday to defend the Trump administration's deportation efforts. Montoya-Galvez joins "The Takeout" to preview the interview.

item.title: In Lynette Hooker probe, Coast Guard seeks info about mystery sailboat
item.description: The U.S. Coast Guard is trying to find the owners of a sailboat that may have been moored next to Lynette Hooker's vessel the night she disappeared in the Bahamas, marking a new development in the search for the missing Michigan woman.

item.title: Full briefing: Marco Rubio says Iran leadership is "insane in the brain"
item.description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio spoke to reporters on Tuesday with an update about the war with Iran. He said, "We are only responding if attacked first," and that Operation Epic Fury is over.

item.title: To avoid risk of mines, Navy directs ships on path farther from Iran
item.description: The U.S. warns that transiting the normal route could be "extremely hazardous" because of mines laid in the Strait of Hormuz by Iran.

item.title: Meta trained its AI on copyrighted work, new lawsuit alleges
item.description: The lawsuit claims that Meta's Llama is generating summaries — and, in some cases, verbatim copies — of original works.

item.title: Iran war will worsen food insecurity in already fragile nations, humanitarian group warns
item.description: The aid organization Mercy Corps is warning that the Iran war will have devastating consequences for nations already on the brink of a humanitarian crisis. Kate Phillips-Barrasso, vice president for global policy and advocacy at Mercy Corps, joins "The Takeout" to discuss.

item.title: Iowa farmer says Trump's "golden age of agriculture" rhetoric "doesn't match the reality"
item.description: Vice President JD Vance is spending Tuesday evening in Iowa stumping for Republican Rep. Zach Nunn. CBS News senior White House and political correspondent Ed O'Keefe reports.

item.title: Rubio says Iran war has shifted to new phase: "Project Freedom"
item.description: On Tuesday, Secretary of State Marco Rubio said the initial phase of the Iran war, dubbed "Operation Epic Fury," is over. He described this latest phase as "Project Freedom." CBS News chief White House correspondent Nancy Cordes reports.

item.title: U.S.-Iran ceasefire tested as U.S. guides ships through Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Hegseth said the ceasefire with Iran "certainly holds" for now after a naval clash between Iranian and U.S. forces and renewed attacks on the UAE.

item.title: What happens on a cruise when a virus breaks out?
item.description: The World Health Organization says the suspected hantavirus outbreak on a cruise ship may have been a rare case of human-to-human transmission. Cruise expert Stewart Chiron joins to explain the protocol on board during viral outbreaks.

item.title: Homan: "Things weren't perfect" in Minneapolis, but ICE not backing down
item.description: Border czar Tom Homan conceded "things weren't perfect" during the crackdown in Minneapolis, but stressed that the administration is not backing down from its mass deportation effort.

item.title: Rubio says Operation Epic Fury is over in Iran
item.description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced the end of Operation Epic Fury in Iran at a news conference on Tuesday. CBS News digital reporter Kathryn Watson has the details.

item.title: Hantavirus possibly transmitted human to human on stricken cruise ship, WHO says
item.description: Passengers aboard the ship are in good spirits but may have to quarantine for "eight weeks," a World Health Organization official told CBS News.

item.title: Border czar Tom Homan acknowledges "things weren't perfect" in Minneapolis crackdown
item.description: Border czar Tom Homan acknowledged "things weren't perfect" in the immigration crackdown in Minneapolis, but highlighted his efforts to make changes after President Trump sent him to the city following the deadly shooting of Alex Pretti. "Things weren't perfect. We addressed it. We fixed it," Homan told CBS News immigration correspondent Camilo Montoya-Galvez in an exclusive interview.

item.title: More companies are pointing to AI as they lay off employees
item.description: Although economists have generally downplayed the impact of artificial intelligence on jobs, some employers are highlighting their adoption of AI.

item.title: Abortions have increased slightly since Roe v. Wade overturning, study finds
item.description: Nearly four years after the Supreme Court overturned Roe v. Wade, a new study found the number of abortions nationwide has slightly increased. The news comes during a renewed legal battle over access to the abortion pill mifepristone. Alina Salganicoff, senior vice president and director of the KFF Women's Health Policy Program, joins CBS News to discuss.

item.title: Tanner Horner sentenced to death in murder of 7-year-old Athena Strand
item.description: A Tarrant County jury sentenced Tanner Horner to death for the 2022 kidnapping and murder of 7-year-old Athena Strand.

item.title: EEOC sues New York Times, alleging bias against White male worker
item.description: Employment watchdog accuses the New York Times of violating federal law by passing over a White male journalist for a job.

item.title: Accused correspondents' dinner shooter charged with assaulting a federal officer
item.description: Accused White House Correspondents' Dinner attacker Cole Allen was indicted Tuesday on four charges, including a new charge of assaulting a federal officer.

item.title: What to know about Tuesday's primaries in Ohio and Indiana
item.description: Voters are going to the polls Tuesday in Ohio and Indiana — in the Buckeye State, they'll be casting ballots in Senate, House and governor's primary races, while Hoosiers will weigh in on House and state races.

item.title: How much interest will a $150,000 money market account earn now (and is it worth opening)?
item.description: Interest earnings on a money market account of this size can be substantial, but is the account worth opening now?

item.title: 2 dead, 3 injured in shooting at Carrollton shopping center, police say
item.description: The incident happened at about 10 a.m. The Carrollton Police Department, Texas Department of Public Safety and other agencies are on scene.

item.title: 2 killed in shooting at Dallas-area shopping center, suspect in custody
item.description: Police gave an update Tuesday on a shooting at a Dallas-area shopping center that killed two people. The suspect is in custody, authorities said.

item.title: Tennessee, Alabama consider congressional redistricting in special sessions
item.description: The nationwide partisan congressional redistricting push has come to Tennessee and Alabama. CBS News' Nicole Valdes and Shawna Mizelle report.

item.title: Delta ending food and beverage service on shorter flights
item.description: Routes that are under a certain distance will no longer offer food and beverage service, the airline said.

item.title: For some older Americans, retirement today means unretiring
item.description: Nearly 20% of Americans of retirement age are employed or seeking employment. "You have to eat," said one 69-year-old of her reasons for continuing to work.

item.title: Education Dept. probing all-women's Smith College for admitting trans women
item.description: The U.S. Department of Education says it's opened an investigation into Smith College, an all-women's institution in Massachusetts, for admitting transgender women.

item.title: Sting embarks on "The Last Ship"
item.description: The English city of Newcastle was hometown of the rock musician Sting, who as a young man witnessed the city's shipbuilding business dry up. He's paid homage to his town's heritage by writing and starring in a musical, "The Last Ship."

item.title: Extended interview: Sting
item.description: In this web exclusive, the rock musician Sting talks with Mark Phillips about his stage musical, "The Last Ship," in which he stars, and which is being performed on a global tour. He calls the show an elegy for what Newcastle and its people represented to him growing up. He also discusses why, for him, uncertainty is a key component of art; why performing "Roxanne" today is never tiresome; and why, for him, music is a church.
